flashcards.q: What is health according to WHO?
flashcards.a: A state of complete physical, mental, and social well-being—not merely the absence of disease.

flashcards.q: Define physical well-being.
flashcards.a: The body functions properly and performs daily tasks efficiently; free from pain and disease.

flashcards.q: Define mental well-being.
flashcards.a: Positive emotions, emotional health, psychological stability, and absence of mental distress.

flashcards.q: Define social well-being.
flashcards.a: Healthy relationships, community connections, and ability to adjust with peers and society.

flashcards.q: What is a disease?
flashcards.a: A condition that affects the normal working of the body or mind; organs or systems stop functioning properly.

flashcards.q: Define a symptom.
flashcards.a: What a person feels internally but cannot be directly seen or measured by others (pain, tiredness, nausea).

flashcards.q: Define a sign.
flashcards.a: An observable or measurable change detected by healthcare providers or instruments (fever, rash, high blood pressure).

flashcards.q: What is a communicable disease?
flashcards.a: A disease caused by pathogens that can spread from one person to another.

flashcards.q: What is a non-communicable disease?
flashcards.a: A disease not caused by pathogens that does not spread between people; linked to lifestyle, diet, genetics.

flashcards.q: Define a pathogen.
flashcards.a: A disease-causing organism including bacteria, viruses, fungi, worms, and protozoa.

flashcards.q: Name four types of pathogens.
flashcards.a: Bacteria, viruses, fungi, and worms (also protozoa).

flashcards.q: List four healthy habits.
flashcards.a: Eat balanced diet, exercise regularly, get adequate sleep, maintain personal hygiene.

flashcards.q: List four harmful habits to avoid.
flashcards.a: Excessive screen time, eating junk food, insufficient sleep, smoking or alcohol use.

flashcards.q: Name three ways communicable diseases spread.
flashcards.a: Airborne (coughing/sneezing), contaminated food/water, direct contact, insect bites.

flashcards.q: What is a vaccine?
flashcards.a: A biological preparation that trains the immune system using weakened, dead, or harmless pathogen parts.

flashcards.q: How do vaccines work?
flashcards.a: They stimulate the immune system to produce antibodies and immunity against specific diseases without causing the disease.

flashcards.q: Are vaccines preventive or curative?
flashcards.a: Preventive—they prevent disease before it occurs, not treat existing infections.

flashcards.q: What is an antibiotic?
flashcards.a: A medicine that kills bacteria and is used to treat bacterial infections.

flashcards.q: Do antibiotics work against viral infections?
flashcards.a: No, antibiotics work only against bacteria, not viruses, fungi, or protozoa.

flashcards.q: Define antibiotic resistance.
flashcards.a: A phenomenon where bacteria survive antibiotic treatment and multiply despite being exposed to that antibiotic.

flashcards.q: What is immunity?
flashcards.a: The natural ability of the body to fight diseases; resistance to pathogens.

flashcards.q: What is the immune system?
flashcards.a: The body's defense system that fights against harmful pathogens and protects against diseases.

flashcards.q: Define natural immunity.
flashcards.a: Inborn resistance to certain diseases that a person is born with.

flashcards.q: Define acquired immunity.
flashcards.a: Protection against disease developed after exposure to a pathogen or through vaccination.

flashcards.q: Name three communicable diseases spread by air.
flashcards.a: Common cold, flu, chickenpox, measles, tuberculosis.

flashcards.q: Name three communicable diseases spread through water/food.
flashcards.a: Typhoid, cholera, hepatitis A, ascariasis (roundworms).

flashcards.q: Name two communicable diseases spread by insect vectors.
flashcards.a: Malaria, dengue fever.

flashcards.q: Name three non-communicable diseases.
flashcards.a: Diabetes, heart disease, cancer, asthma, obesity.

flashcards.q: What are deficiency diseases?
flashcards.a: Diseases caused by lack of specific nutrients in diet; examples: scurvy, anaemia, goitre.

flashcards.q: Name the SI unit of health measurement.
flashcards.a: There is no single unit; health is measured using multiple indicators (vaccination rates, disease prevalence, mortality rates).

flashcards.q: Fill in: Vaccines prevent disease _____.
flashcards.a: Before it occurs (preventive medicine).

flashcards.q: Fill in: Antibiotics treat disease _____.
flashcards.a: After infection develops (curative medicine).

flashcards.q: Fill in: _____ is the force that causes bacteria to develop resistance.
flashcards.a: Antibiotic exposure/selection pressure.

flashcards.q: Fill in: Edward Jenner discovered the first _____ using cowpox material.
flashcards.a: Vaccine.

flashcards.q: Fill in: _____ is the upright practice in India for smallpox immunity before modern vaccines.
flashcards.a: Variolation.

flashcards.q: Fill in: A _____ is an organism that carries and transmits disease pathogens.
flashcards.a: Vector.

flashcards.q: Fill in: _____ are organisms that live in or on another living being and feed on nutrients.
flashcards.a: Parasites.

flashcards.q: Fill in: Handwashing reduces infection transmission by _____.
Fill in: The menstrual cycle occurs every _____ days in healthy women.
True or False: Health is just the absence of disease.
flashcards.a: False; health includes physical, mental, and social well-being.

flashcards.q: True or False: All diseases are caused by pathogens.
flashcards.a: False; non-communicable diseases are not caused by pathogens.

flashcards.q: True or False: Antibiotics work against viral infections.
flashcards.a: False; antibiotics specifically kill bacteria, not viruses.

flashcards.q: True or False: Vaccines cause the disease they prevent.
flashcards.a: False; vaccines use weakened/dead pathogens or harmless parts; they don't cause disease.

flashcards.q: True or False: Antibiotic resistance develops naturally in bacteria.
flashcards.a: True; some bacteria naturally have resistance; antibiotic exposure selects for resistant bacteria.

flashcards.q: True or False: Mental health is as important as physical health.
flashcards.a: True; poor mental health affects overall health; emotional distress causes physical symptoms.

flashcards.q: True or False: Communicable diseases can spread from person to person.
flashcards.a: True.

flashcards.q: True or False: Non-communicable diseases spread through pathogens.
flashcards.a: False; they are caused by lifestyle, genetics, and environment—not pathogens.

flashcards.q: True or False: Vaccines are curative medicine.
flashcards.a: False; vaccines are preventive—prevent disease before exposure.

flashcards.q: True or False: You should complete the full course of antibiotics even if you feel better.
flashcards.a: True; stopping early allows bacteria to survive and develop resistance.

flashcards.q: What percentage of type 2 diabetes is preventable with lifestyle changes?
flashcards.a: Approximately 75%.

flashcards.q: When was penicillin, the first antibiotic, discovered?
flashcards.a: 1928 by Alexander Fleming.

flashcards.q: In what year was smallpox eradicated?
What was India's major health achievement in 2014?
flashcards.a: Certified polio-free.

flashcards.q: Name the government health initiative promoting physical activity in India.
flashcards.a: Fit India Movement.

flashcards.q: What is the most common type of modern rechargeable battery?
flashcards.a: This question is not relevant to Health chapter; skip.

flashcards.q: Which group of people have stronger immunity naturally?
flashcards.a: Young, healthy adults with good nutrition and sleep.

flashcards.q: Fill in: To prevent antibiotic resistance, use antibiotics only when _____.
flashcards.a: Prescribed by doctors, in correct doses, for full duration.

flashcards.q: Fill in: The WHO definition of health includes _____, _____, and _____ well-being.
flashcards.a: Physical, mental, social.

flashcards.q: What is Ayurveda's approach to health?
flashcards.a: Balance of body, mind, and surroundings through dinacharya, ritucharya, yoga, meditation.

flashcards.q: Name two traditional Indian practices for health maintenance.
flashcards.a: Yoga, meditation, pranayama (breathing exercises), Ayurvedic practices.

flashcards.q: True or False: Vaccines have saved millions of lives.
flashcards.a: True; vaccines save millions of lives yearly.

flashcards.q: True or False: Environment affects our health.
flashcards.a: True; clean environment, pure water, clean air are essential for health.

flashcards.q: True or False: Symptoms and signs are the same thing.
flashcards.a: False; symptoms are felt internally; signs are observable.

flashcards.q: Fill in: Malaria and dengue are spread by _____.
flashcards.a: Mosquitoes.

flashcards.q: Fill in: Cholera and typhoid are caused by _____.
flashcards.a: Bacteria.

flashcards.q: Fill in: Common cold and flu are caused by _____.
flashcards.a: Viruses.

flashcards.q: Name three ways to prevent communicable disease spread.
flashcards.a: Handwashing, covering mouth/nose, staying home when unwell, clean water/food, vaccination.

flashcards.q: What is the first step when starting a prescribed antibiotic?
flashcards.a: Take exactly as prescribed; complete the full course regardless of feeling better.

flashcards.q: True or False: Loneliness can make us physically sick.
flashcards.a: True; mental/social well-being directly affects physical health.

flashcards.q: Fill in: The region around a magnet where its effect is felt is called a _____.
flashcards.a: This question is not relevant to Health chapter; skip.
